title: The 'Looksmaxxing' and Skincare Obsession: When Social Media Trends Fuel High School Anxiety
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have seen the videos. A teenager with flawless skin explains their ten-step skincare routine. Another analyzes the bone structure of a male model and prescribes exercises to sharpen a jawline. A third ranks facial features on a numerical scale and offers advice for improving your score. These are not fringe communities. They are among the most popular content categories on platforms used by millions of teenagers. The aesthetics are polished. The advice is delivered with confidence. The underlying message is that your appearance is a problem to be solved, and the solution is a combination of products, procedures, and self-discipline. Here is what these trends are doing to high school students and how to think about them critically.

What These Trends Actually Are

The term looksmaxxing originated in online communities dedicated to maximizing physical attractiveness. The practice involves analyzing one's appearance in detail and taking steps to improve it. Some of these steps are harmless. Better skincare. Exercise. A flattering haircut. Others are extreme. Mewing, a technique of pressing the tongue against the roof of the mouth to supposedly reshape the jawline, has no scientific support and can cause dental problems. Bone smashing, the practice of applying pressure to facial bones to change their shape, is dangerous and can cause permanent damage. Some communities promote unregulated supplements, extreme dieting, or surgical procedures to minors.

The skincare obsession is a parallel but distinct trend. Driven by influencers, many of whom are teenagers themselves, it promotes elaborate routines involving multiple products. Cleansers, toners, serums, moisturizers, sunscreens, exfoliants, retinoids. The routines are expensive. The products are marketed to concerns that many teenagers did not have before they were told they should have them. A fifteen-year-old with normal skin is told they need anti-aging products. A sixteen-year-old with occasional acne is told their skin is damaged and requires an intensive regimen to fix.

The common thread is the medicalization of normal appearance. Features that are part of being a teenager, acne, oily skin, a face that is still developing, are reframed as defects requiring treatment. The treatment is available for purchase. The influencers selling it are often paid by the brands they promote.

The Psychological Toll

These trends land hard on teenagers because adolescence is already a period of intense self-consciousness. The body is changing. Social hierarchies are being navigated. Identity is being formed. The normal developmental challenges of being a teenager are difficult enough without being told that your face needs fixing.

Research on social media and body image consistently finds that exposure to idealized images increases dissatisfaction with one's own appearance. The effect is stronger when the images come from peers rather than celebrities. An influencer who looks like you but with better skin, a sharper jawline, and a more symmetrical face is more damaging to your self-image than an unattainable movie star. The comparison feels more personal. The gap between their appearance and yours feels closeable, which makes the dissatisfaction sharper.

Obsessive focus on appearance also consumes mental bandwidth that could go elsewhere. Hours spent researching skincare routines, analyzing facial features, and comparing yourself to others are hours not spent on schoolwork, hobbies, friendships, or sleep. The appearance obsession crowds out the activities that actually contribute to well-being. The pursuit of a perfect appearance makes you less happy, not more.

What Parents and Students Should Know

If you are a student caught in these trends, understand that the people promoting them have financial incentives. The influencer with the ten-step skincare routine is often paid by the brands whose products appear in the video. The account promoting looksmaxxing techniques may be selling coaching services or supplements. The advice is not neutral. It is advertising. The goal is to make you feel inadequate so you will buy something that promises to fix the inadequacy.

Your face is still developing. Teenage bone structure is not adult bone structure. The jawline you have at sixteen is not the jawline you will have at twenty-two. Procedures and extreme practices undertaken during development can have permanent consequences that you cannot undo. Do not let social media pressure you into altering a face that is not finished growing.

If skincare is genuinely a problem, acne that causes pain or scarring, see a dermatologist. A medical professional can provide treatments that work. The influencer's routine is designed to sell products, not to treat skin conditions. A dermatologist's advice is based on evidence. The difference matters.

Limit your exposure to appearance-focused content. The algorithms show you more of what you engage with. If you watch skincare routines and looksmaxxing content, your feed will fill with it. The more you see, the more normal it seems. The more normal it seems, the more dissatisfied you become with your own appearance. Break the cycle by changing what you engage with. Follow accounts about your hobbies, your interests, your goals. Fill your feed with things that make you feel capable, not inadequate.

The Bottom Line

The looksmaxxing and skincare obsession on social media is driven by influencers with financial incentives to make you feel dissatisfied with your appearance. Teenagers are especially vulnerable because adolescence is already a period of self-consciousness and identity formation. The obsessive focus on appearance consumes mental bandwidth, damages self-image, and can lead to dangerous practices. Understand the commercial motives behind the content. See a dermatologist for genuine skin concerns. Limit your exposure to appearance-focused media. Your face is still developing. Give it time.
